title: SEGMENT 3 — The four ingredients: a flow cell, bridge amplification, reversible terminators, a camera

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The four ingredients: a flow cell, bridge amplification, reversible terminators, a camera
Every Illumina instrument, from the smallest iSeq to the largest NovaSeq X, runs on the same four core components.
If you understand these four things, you understand the platform.
Everything else is engineering scale — making each component bigger, denser, faster, cheaper — but the fundamental architecture is constant.
So let me lay out the four ingredients, and then we'll spend the next several segments going deep on each.
Ingredient one: a flow cell.
The flow cell is a small piece of glass, about the size of a microscope slide, with a series of channels etched into it.
Through these channels you can flow reagents — nucleotides, enzymes, wash buffers — and on the inner surface of the channels, the actual sequencing happens.
The flow cell is the stage where everything takes place.

It is the most underappreciated component of the system, because all the chemistry needs to work on its inner surface, in repeated cycles, without contamination or degradation, sometimes for forty-eight hours straight.
Think of it as a microscope slide ruled with billions of anchor points where DNA fragments stick.
Each anchor point is the start of a single sequencing reaction.
A NovaSeq X flow cell has on the order of fifty billion such anchor points.
That's an absurd density.
It's why throughput per run is what it is.
Ingredient two: bridge amplification.
Here's the problem.
A single DNA molecule, with a single fluorescent dye attached to one new base, gives off only a tiny amount of light when illuminated.
Way too little for a camera to reliably detect at the speeds we need.
So before you start reading, you take each DNA fragment and you make about a thousand identical copies of it, all clustered together in one tiny spot on the flow cell.
When the polymerase adds a new base in that spot, all thousand molecules incorporate the same base at the same time, and all thousand dyes light up together.
The signal from a cluster is a thousand times brighter than from a single molecule — bright enough for a camera to pick up cleanly.
The technique that produces these clusters is called bridge amplification, and we'll spend Segment 5 on it.
For now, the picture: each starting DNA molecule grows into a cluster of about a thousand identical copies, like a colony of clones standing next to each other.
You read the cluster's signal rather than the individual molecule's.
That's the second ingredient.
Ingredient three: reversible terminator chemistry.
We met this in Segment 2.
The four nucleotides, each carrying a distinct fluorescent dye and a chemical blocker that prevents the polymerase from adding more than one base per cycle.
Add the nucleotides, let the polymerase incorporate one, take a photograph to record the color, chemically cleave off the dye and the blocker, repeat.
This is the actual sequencing chemistry.
One base per cycle, color-coded, with a built-in brake.
That's ingredient three.
Ingredient four: a camera and a light source.
After each cycle of incorporation, the instrument illuminates the flow cell with laser light at specific wavelengths and takes a series of photographs through a high-resolution microscope.
Each photograph captures the fluorescence emitted by the clusters.
The instrument moves the camera across the flow cell, tile by tile, photographing the entire surface.
From these photographs, software extracts the color at each cluster's position in each cycle, and from that color, it infers which base was just added.
Take a photo, read out the color of every cluster, record what base each cluster just incorporated.
That's ingredient four.
So here is the picture you should now have.
A glass slide, ruled with billions of microscopic anchor points.
At each anchor point, a tiny cluster of about a thousand identical DNA molecules, all derived from one starting fragment.
Reagents flowing across the surface, delivering blocked fluorescent nucleotides.
After each delivery, a wash, a flash of laser light, a photograph through the microscope, recording which color shone at each cluster's position.
Then a chemical cleavage step to remove the dye and the blocker, getting the molecules ready for the next round.
Repeat this cycle one hundred and fifty times, or three hundred times, depending on how long a read you want.

At the end, the instrument has accumulated a stack of one hundred and fifty photographs of the same flow cell, and at each cluster's position, a sequence of one hundred and fifty colors — which is to say, a sequence of one hundred and fifty bases.
Run this on a billion clusters in parallel and you have a hundred and fifty billion base pairs of sequence data, which is roughly fifty times the size of the human genome, in about a day.
The four-step cycle at the heart of all this — incorporate, image, cleave, repeat — is the master rhythm of Illumina sequencing.
Memorize it.
Sketch it.
Hold it in your head.
If you understand incorporate-image-cleave-repeat, multiplied by parallelism on a flow cell, you have the whole platform in compressed form.
The rest of this lecture is just unpacking each of those four words and explaining the engineering required to make them work at scale.
Let's go through them in order.
Library prep first, because before any of this can happen, you have to get your DNA into a form the flow cell can recognize.
